# Philosophy of Design

Good software is built through careful thought and deliberate choices. Every line of code represents a decision, and the best decisions come from understanding both the problem and the context in which the solution will operate.

## Simplicity

The most powerful designs are often the simplest. Complexity should be introduced only when it earns its place, and even then, it should be isolated and well-documented. A system that is easy to understand is easy to maintain, extend, and debug.

When faced with a choice between a clever solution and a straightforward one, choose straightforward. Cleverness impresses in the moment but confuses in the future. The person reading your code six months from now — who may well be you — will thank you for choosing clarity over cleverness.

## Consistency

Consistency reduces cognitive load. When patterns repeat predictably, readers can focus on what is different rather than re-learning how each piece works. This applies to naming, structure, error handling, and documentation.

A codebase that follows consistent conventions is greater than the sum of its parts. Each piece reinforces the patterns established by the others, creating a coherent whole that is easier to navigate than any individual file.

## Pragmatism

Perfection is the enemy of progress. Ship working software, then improve it. The best architecture is one that solves today's problems while leaving room for tomorrow's changes, without over-engineering for hypothetical futures.

Make decisions based on evidence and experience, not dogma. Every rule has exceptions, and the wisdom lies in knowing when to follow the rule and when to break it.
